other_observers: Joe Roller and Glenn Walbek; can't remember which of us spotted it first, but Joe was the first to put a name to it.
specific_location: Cherry Creek State Park, at the swim beach
description: the bird was a medium-sized warbler, about the size of nearby Yellow-rumped Warblers. It was fairly "chunky" in shape. The bill was black, quite long, straight, and thick (for a warbler; just a little less than the thickness of the eye). The legs were black, with the "soles" of the feet a fleshy yellow color.  The bird's cap, auriculars, and back were a grayish-brown color, with a very, very slight yellowish or greenish tinge, barely noticeable. The bird had a bold white split eye-ring and a fainter, dusky white supraloral. The sides of the neck, extending onto the nape just behind the grayish-brown auricular, was a slightly but noticeably paler grayish-brown. The bird's throat was off-white with a slight buffy tinge, especially on the sides of the throat, with the center of the throat being almost pure white. The bird's chest had a buffy wash across it, and the sides and flanks had the same buffy wash, though paler, and fading near the undertail coverts. There were also some very faint and indistinct streaks on the lower flanks. The belly and undertail coverts were white. The wings were dark gray, with two somewhat bold wingbars that were slightly off-white. The bird had a long tail that projected well beyond the undertail coverts, and and was mostly white below, with small dark gray tail corners and a small area of dark gray at the base.Behaviors: the bird was a mixed feeding flock moving around in the upper parts of small locust trees in the area.Call: none heardPlumage: first basic
